title: Spring Boot
objectives: Understand Spring Boot via a real-life analogy
Know the core building blocks: Beans, IoC, `@RestController`, `@Service`, `@Repository`
Configure profiles, application properties, and DI properly
Wire a REST endpoint to JPA with transactions
Answer Spring Boot interview questions confidently

sections.markdown:
**Think of Spring Boot as a fully-furnished apartment vs an empty warehouse.**

- **Plain Spring** was an empty warehouse ã powerful, flexible, but you had to install every electrical outlet, hang every light, wire every plumbing yourself. XML configuration was thousands of lines. 'Hello world' took an afternoon.
- **Spring Boot** is a fully-furnished apartment ã the kitchen is stocked, the lights work, the internet's on. You walk in and start LIVING. To add a REST endpoint you write ONE class. To add a database you list ONE dependency. Sensible defaults everywhere; overrides where you need them.

**Concretely:** Spring Boot is Spring + convention-over-configuration + an embedded server + starter dependencies. `mvn spring-boot:run` and your app is live on port 8080. Add `spring-boot-starter-web` ã you get Tomcat + JSON + validation. Add `spring-boot-starter-data-jpa` ã you get connection pooling + Hibernate + transaction management. Zero XML, minimal config.

**Why it dominates:** every enterprise Java backend written in the last 10 years is Spring Boot. Every microservice on JVM. Every Kotlin backend. It's the JVM's answer to Ruby on Rails or Django ã but arguably more powerful, less opinionated, better for large teams. The trade-off: it's HUGE. There are 200+ Spring projects; the docs are a book; runtime magic (`@Autowired`, `@Transactional`) can confuse. Learn the CORE (IoC, Beans, controllers, JPA, config) ã that's 90% of daily use.

**Spring Boot** is a framework that makes it easy to build production-ready Spring applications with minimal configuration. Built on **Spring Framework** (IoC container + DI + AOP), it adds: **starter dependencies** (`spring-boot-starter-web` pulls in a working web stack), **auto-configuration** (Spring figures out what beans to create based on the classpath), **embedded server** (Tomcat/Jetty runs inside your JAR ã no external app server), and **`application.yaml`/`.properties`** configuration. Standard for enterprise Java backends since ~2014.
## The core building blocks

rows: Marks the main class; enables auto-config, component-scan, config properties
Marks a class as a REST controller (`@Controller` + `@ResponseBody`)
Marks a data-access bean; adds exception translation
Any bean not fitting the others
Inject a bean by type (also works via constructor)
Bind a whole config section into a typed class
Wrap method in a DB transaction

sections.markdown: ## Sample program ã a complete REST + JPA slice
## Common mistakes

**Mistake 1: Field injection with `@Autowired` on fields.** `@Autowired private UserService svc;` ã mutable, hides deps, hard to test. Use CONSTRUCTOR injection (Spring 4.3+ makes `@Autowired` optional on single constructors).
**Mistake 2: Business logic in controllers.** Controllers should be THIN ã parse request, call service, return response. Business rules live in `@Service` beans, testable in isolation.
**Mistake 3: `@Transactional` on controllers.** Transactions belong on Service methods. Controllers have no business managing DB lifetimes.
**Mistake 4: Exposing JPA entities as JSON.** `return userRepo.findById(id);` ã leaks internal fields, eagerly fetches lazy relations, breaks encapsulation. Always convert to a DTO.
**Mistake 5: Ignoring profiles.** `dev`, `test`, `prod` profiles let you swap config cleanly. `spring.profiles.active=prod` picks `application-prod.yaml` overrides.
**Mistake 6: `ddl-auto: create-drop` in production.** Wipes the DB on every restart. Use `validate` in prod; migrations via Flyway or Liquibase.
## Best practices
**1. Constructor injection.** Explicit, testable, immutable.
**2. Thin controllers, fat services.** Controllers translate HTTP; services own logic.
**3. DTOs at the boundary.** Never return JPA entities directly.
**4. `@Transactional` on services.** Not repositories, not controllers.
**5. Profiles for environments.** `dev`, `test`, `prod` ã each with its own `application-*.yaml`.
**6. Flyway/Liquibase for DB migrations.** Version-controlled SQL; `ddl-auto: validate` in prod.
**7. Test with `@SpringBootTest` + Testcontainers.** Real Postgres in tests; no H2 fakery.
**8. Actuator for ops.** `/actuator/health`, `/actuator/metrics`, `/actuator/info` ã free monitoring endpoints.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) ã Hello World endpoint.** `@GetMapping("/hello")` returning `"Hello, world!"`. Run with `bootRun`.
**Practice 2 (Medium) ã User CRUD.** Build the sample. Test with curl / Postman.
**Practice 3 (Medium) ã Add validation.** `@NotBlank`, `@Email` on request records; verify 400 with field errors on invalid input.
**Practice 4 (Hard) ã Profiles + config.** `application-dev.yaml` uses H2; `application-prod.yaml` uses Postgres. `spring.profiles.active=dev` selects.
**Practice 5 (Hard) ã Flyway migrations.** `V1__create_users.sql` + `V2__add_deleted_at.sql`. Deploy without downtime.
**Practice 6 (Expert) ã Testcontainers.** Real Postgres in `@SpringBootTest`; each test spins up a fresh container.
## Interview Q&A ã with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is Spring Boot and how does it differ from Spring?**

**A:** Spring is the underlying framework ã IoC container, dependency injection, AOP, transaction management, MVC. Powerful but historically required massive XML/Java configuration. Spring Boot is a wrapper around Spring that provides: **(1) starter dependencies** (e.g., `spring-boot-starter-web` pulls in Tomcat, Jackson, validation), **(2) auto-configuration** (Spring examines the classpath and creates default beans), **(3) embedded server** (Tomcat/Jetty run INSIDE your JAR ã no external app server), **(4) `application.yaml/properties`** conventions, **(5) production features** (Actuator for health/metrics). Result: 'hello world' in 3 files instead of 30. Every modern Java backend is Spring Boot.

**Q2: What is Inversion of Control and Dependency Injection?**

**A:** **IoC (Inversion of Control)** ã instead of your code CREATING its dependencies (`new UserRepository()`), a container (Spring's `ApplicationContext`) creates them and hands them to you. Control of object creation is inverted from your code to the framework. **DI (Dependency Injection)** ã the mechanism: dependencies are PROVIDED (via constructor / setter / field), not fetched. Spring reads your `@Component`/`@Service` classes at startup, builds a graph of beans, and injects each into the ones that need it. **Result:** classes depend on interfaces (testable), one place wires them together, swappable in tests. This is the beating heart of Spring ã everything else builds on it. Interview: mention that DI is HOW you achieve IoC.

**Q3: `@Component` vs `@Service` vs `@Repository` vs `@Controller`?**

**A:** All four are variants of `@Component` ã they mark classes as Spring-managed beans. Differences are semantic + minor behavior: **`@Component`** ã generic bean. **`@Service`** ã business logic (semantically distinct; no extra behavior). **`@Repository`** ã data-access bean; adds **exception translation** (JPA `PersistenceException` ã Spring `DataAccessException`). **`@Controller`** ã MVC controller returning views. **`@RestController`** ã `@Controller` + `@ResponseBody` ã every method returns JSON. **Rule:** use the most specific annotation; Spring can adjust behavior based on it (e.g., transactions, exception translation). Interview: emphasize `@RestController` for REST APIs and `@Repository` for JPA classes.

**Q4: How does Spring Boot's auto-configuration work?**

**A:** Spring Boot scans the classpath and creates default beans based on what it finds. Example: **classpath has `spring-boot-starter-web`?** ã auto-create a Tomcat server, register Jackson for JSON, add validators. **classpath has `spring-boot-starter-data-jpa`?** ã auto-configure a connection pool (HikariCP), an EntityManagerFactory, transaction manager. Auto-configuration is driven by `@Conditional*` annotations ã 'create THIS bean only if THAT class is on the classpath AND no bean of this type exists yet'. Your own `@Bean` methods override auto-configured defaults. **`spring-boot-starter-*` dependencies** are the trigger ã each pulls the right stack for one concern. This is why 'add a dep, get a feature' works so well.

**Q5: How do transactions work in Spring?**

**A:** `@Transactional` wraps a method in a database transaction ã BEGIN before the method, COMMIT on normal return, ROLLBACK on uncaught runtime exception. Implemented via **AOP** ã Spring creates a proxy around your bean; the proxy handles begin/commit/rollback around each `@Transactional` method call. **Gotchas:** **(1) Self-invocation doesn't trigger the proxy** ã calling `this.method()` bypasses `@Transactional`. Refactor to a separate bean. **(2) Only `RuntimeException` triggers rollback** by default, NOT checked exceptions ã configure with `rollbackFor=Exception.class`. **(3) Propagation** ã default is `REQUIRED` (join existing txn if any); `REQUIRES_NEW` starts a fresh one. **(4) Read-only** ã mark with `@Transactional(readOnly = true)` for GETs (small performance win).

**Q6: What are Spring profiles?**

**A:** A way to activate different configuration per environment. `spring.profiles.active=prod` ã Spring loads `application-prod.yaml` on top of `application.yaml`. `dev`, `test`, `prod` are conventions. Also gates beans: `@Profile("prod")` on a bean makes it exist only in that profile. **Use cases:** different DB URLs per env, different logging, dev-only mocks, prod-only monitoring beans. **Setting the profile:** `-Dspring.profiles.active=prod`, `SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod` env var, or `spring.profiles.active` in properties. **Rule:** never hard-code environment values; profiles + env vars = clean separation. Use `dev`, `test`, `prod` at minimum; some teams add `staging`, `local`.

**Q7: How do you test a Spring Boot app?**

**A:** Three levels. **(1) Unit tests** ã plain JUnit, no Spring. Inject fakes into services; test business logic in milliseconds. Fast, hermetic. **(2) `@WebMvcTest` / `@DataJpaTest`** ã slice tests. Spin up ONLY the controller layer or DB layer. Faster than full context. **(3) `@SpringBootTest`** ã full application context. Ideal for integration tests. Combine with **Testcontainers** to run real Postgres/Redis/Kafka in Docker containers per test class. **Best practice:** unit tests for services (95%), slice tests for controllers/repositories (4%), full integration tests for critical paths (1%). Avoid the 'test everything with `@SpringBootTest`' anti-pattern ã startup time kills your test suite.

**Q8: What is Spring Actuator and why is it useful?**

**A:** Spring Boot Actuator adds production-ready operational endpoints ã `/actuator/health` (is the app alive?), `/actuator/metrics` (memory, GC, HTTP counters), `/actuator/info` (build metadata), `/actuator/prometheus` (for Prometheus scraping), `/actuator/loggers` (change log level at runtime), `/actuator/env` (view current config). All auto-configured ã just add `spring-boot-starter-actuator`. **Why:** every prod service needs a `/health` endpoint for K8s liveness/readiness probes. Every prod service needs metrics for observability. Actuator gives you both without writing them. **Security:** endpoints are sensitive by default; expose only `/health` publicly, protect the rest via Spring Security. Actuator alone justifies using Spring Boot over a hand-rolled framework.

## Real-world usage

- **Every enterprise Java backend** in the last 10 years.
- **Netflix, Alibaba, PayPal, Airbnb** ã all heavy Spring Boot users.
- **Kotlin backends** ã Spring Boot supports Kotlin natively.
- **Microservices** ã the dominant JVM microservice framework.
- **Reactive stacks** ã Spring WebFlux for non-blocking (alternative to Spring MVC).
- **Cloud-native** ã Spring Cloud adds service discovery, config server, circuit breakers.
- **Alternatives** ã Quarkus, Micronaut (faster startup for serverless), Ktor (Kotlin-idiomatic).

## Advanced notes

- **`@Async`** + `@EnableAsync` ã background execution via a task executor.
- **`@Scheduled`** ã cron-style scheduled tasks.
- **`ApplicationEventPublisher`** ã in-process pub/sub between beans.
- **`ApplicationRunner` / `CommandLineRunner`** ã code that runs at startup.
- **`WebClient`** (WebFlux) ã reactive HTTP client, replaces RestTemplate.
- **`RestTemplate` / `RestClient`** ã synchronous HTTP clients.
- **Spring WebFlux** ã reactive stack (Netty, Mono/Flux); best for high-concurrency I/O.
- **Spring Security** ã auth + authz; complex but powerful. See its own topic.
- **Spring Cloud** ã service discovery (Eureka), config server, circuit breaker (Resilience4j), gateway.
- **`@ConditionalOnProperty`** ã bean creation gated by property value.
- **`spring-boot-devtools`** ã hot reload in dev.
- **JMX + Actuator** ã legacy but still useful.
- **Spring AOT / native image** ã GraalVM native compilation for faster startup.

flashcards.back: Spring + convention-over-configuration + starter deps + embedded server + auto-configuration.
IoC = framework creates dependencies. DI = provided via constructor/setter/field. Result: testable, decoupled.
Self-invocation bypasses proxy. Only RuntimeException triggers rollback by default. Put on services, not controllers.

quiz.explain: Starters bring deps; conditions gate beans.
Production ops out of the box.

challenges.text: Register a @Bean for a Clock; inject in another bean.
User CRUD with JPA + validation + error handler.
Add dev/prod profiles with different DB URLs.
Flyway migrations V1..V3 with data seed.
@Scheduled task that runs every 5 minutes; use profile to disable in tests.
